title: #eng-platform: 1738956625
preview:
Ethan Park: Trying to lock rollout sequencing for audit logging v1 so we don’t thrash across teams. Proposal: (1) schema lib + validator, (2) control plane producers (RBAC + API keys), (3) console backend + UI workflows, (4) private installer parity. Thoughts / gotchas?

Priya Natarajan: +1 on sequencing. The dependency graph is real: everyone needs the schema + event_type registry first or we’ll ship 4 shapes. Let’s make it explicit as a gate for downstream PRs.

Connor O'Brien: For release coordination: can we define “Phase gates” w/ checkboxes? I need something I can paste into the release train notes.

Ethan Park: Yep. Gate 0: merged schema library + JSON Schema artifact + validator. Gate 1: control plane emitting v1 events for RBAC + API key lifecycle behind feature flag (per-tenant). Gate 2: console emits/validates and UI flows audited. Gate 3: private installer emits install/upgrade/rollback/config-apply events.

Sahana Iyer: For Gate 0, we should also land CI lints early, even if “warn-only” initially. Otherwise folks will add new event types ad-hoc while we’re mid-migration.

Ethan Park: Agree. But please don’t block everyone day 1 — maybe warn-only for 1 week then enforce?

Marcus Lin: (dropping in) I’m aligned with warn-only → enforce. But we need an explicit date/commit for the switch, and an exception process (time-boxed) so we don’t end up with permanent carve-outs.

Diego Santos: From the parity angle: sequencing should minimize “Hosted emits event X but Private doesn’t” windows. If we do control plane first, that covers the biggest customer asks (RBAC + keys). But we need to communicate that Private parity is coming and how long.

Dylan Brooks: Console note: we’re currently attributing actor in 2 different ways (session user vs service token) depending on workflow. If schema lib lands first, we can normalize actor + impersonation fields before we stamp more events.

Chloe Martin: +1. Also, console backend is a major producer for “export config changes” + “retention/residency settings”. Those depend on the same actor attribution fixes.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: Auth/authz detail: control plane RBAC + API keys are the most sensitive, but they’re also the easiest to get wrong in terms of “actor vs subject”. Please make sure the schema library includes an explicit pattern for: actor (human/service), subject (user affected), and target resource.

Ethan Park: That’s in the latest draft. Actor = who initiated; subject = user/service account affected; target = resource being modified. For key events, never log key material, only key_id + last4 if we have it.

Leila Farouk: Testing sequencing: if Gate 0 doesn’t include fixtures/examples, Gate 1+ will emit “valid schema” but we won’t catch semantics regressions. Can we include example payloads + golden tests in the schema lib PR?

Sahana Iyer: +1. Golden tests also help CI enforce required fields without devs guessing.

Priya Natarajan: Risk mitigation list we should capture:
- Feature flag per tenant (and per deployment mode) so we can roll back producers without rolling back core services
- Exporter normalization path for legacy events so customers don’t get broken dashboards during transition
- Cardinality guardrails (allow-lists) before console starts emitting free-form strings
- Backpressure behavior defined (don’t block admin workflows if audit sink is down)

Ethan Park: On backpressure: we’re leaning at-least-once delivery in pipeline, but producer behavior is best-effort (don’t fail the request if audit publish fails). We’ll emit metrics + alerts for publish failures.

Marcus Lin: Make sure that’s consistent across Hosted/Dedicated/Private. We’ve had past “private blocks install if telemetry down” surprises.

Diego Santos: For Private installer, we also need an offline-friendly buffer. Some customer installs have restricted egress. If export destination is unreachable, we should spool locally w/ bounded disk + explicit operator warnings.

Connor O'Brien: Can we put that in Gate 3 acceptance criteria? “Installer continues; audit events queued; admin sees warning; events flushed when export configured.”

Ethan Park: Yes.

Dylan Brooks: Console risk: we have multiple UI entry points for the same action (e.g., role edit from user page vs RBAC page). If we instrument UI + BE, we may double-emit unless we decide “only backend emits” for state changes.

Chloe Martin: Strong preference: backend emits for anything state-changing. UI emits only “view/access” events if we decide those are in scope later. For this project, sensitive actions are mostly mutations.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: +1. If we double-emit now, customers will scream “duplicates” and we’ll be back in dedupe land.

Sahana Iyer: That ties to event_id/idempotency. If backend is the source of truth, generate event_id server-side; if exporter retries, it should preserve event_id so SIEM dedupe is possible.

Ethan Park: Yup. We’ll standardize event_id generation in the library.

Priya Natarajan: Proposed concrete sequencing / timeline (rough):
Week 1: schema lib + registry + examples + warn-only lints
Week 2-3: control plane (RBAC + API keys) + integration tests
Week 3-4: console backend producers + actor attribution cleanup
Week 4-6: private installer parity + buffering guidance
Parallel: exporter normalization + schema_version tagging

Connor O'Brien: This is perfect. I’ll convert to release gates with owners.

Ethan Park: Owners suggestion:
Gate 0: Ethan + Sahana
Gate 1: Ethan + Kaitlyn (actor semantics)
Gate 2: Dylan + Chloe
Gate 3: Diego + Vivek’s team (I’ll loop them)

Leila Farouk: Please add “Gate N: E2E tests in CI” as a cross-cutting item. We can stage them: hosted fixture first, private fixture second.

Marcus Lin: Also add “rollback plan” per gate. If Gate 2 causes unexpected volume/cardinality, we need a kill switch.

Dylan Brooks: We can add a config toggle in console backend to drop audit publish (still log metrics) for emergency.

Priya Natarajan: Great. Capture decisions + mitigations in the rollout doc and link here.

Ethan Park: Will do. I’ll post the draft plan in Confluence + ping for :eyes: today.

Connor O'Brien: :thumbsup: (reaction)

Sahana Iyer: :white_check_mark: (reaction)

Marcus Lin: Thanks all. Let’s keep scope discipline — schema first, then producers. No bespoke fields without registry approval.

Ethan Park: +1. Closing the loop after I post the plan link.

(DeployBot): Reminder: CI “audit-event-lint (warn-only)” pipeline is available on branch `audit/schema-lib-rollout` (docs: go/redwood/audit-lint).

title: #eng-platform: 1738948822
preview:
Ethan Park: Dropping this here for review before we merge the backup status API. PR is up: redwood/pr-24318 ("Expose backup status endpoints for Private"). Main thing: endpoint to surface `last_success_ts` etc so Console + automation can show DR readiness.

GitHub Bot: @eng-platform PR #24318 ready for review. Checks: unit-tests ✅ lint ✅

Priya Natarajan: Skimmed. The shape looks fine, but I’m worried about authz + tenancy isolation. Right now it’s `GET /private/v1/backups/status?tenant_id=...` (from the PR description). We should not accept tenant_id as a free param.

Benji Okafor: Yep, in code it’s `tenant_id` pulled from the JWT claims if present, param is only used by internal calls. But I agree it’s confusing.

Marcus Lin: +1 to Priya. Even if “internal”, params have a way of sticking around. Strong preference:
- derive tenant/install identity from auth context (mTLS client cert / service token / admin session)
- don’t allow a caller to enumerate tenants by guessing IDs
- if we need an admin override, make it a separate admin-only route behind `private:admin` scope.

Hanae Suzuki: Also, what’s the expected caller here in Private?
- Console inside the customer VPC?
- our support tooling?
Because token scope / audience needs to be explicit. We shouldn’t accidentally allow data-plane workload identities to call this.

Ethan Park: Intended callers:
1) Private Console backend (same control plane namespace)
2) Installer/automation (cron) to post status
3) SRE tooling in Redwood-managed staging
Not intended for customer apps.

Sean Gallagher: For SRE: we’ll likely scrape/poll this (or have metrics). If endpoint is cheap and rate limited, ok. But if it hits DB every time, we’ll DDOS ourselves with dashboards.

Allison Grant: From observability POV, I’d rather we have metrics emitted by the backup job and only use the API for “human view” / support diagnostics. But I get why Console wants it.

Priya Natarajan: Proposal:
- Route: `GET /private/v1/backup/status` (singular “this install”) w/ no identifiers.
- Response includes: `last_success_ts`, `last_attempt_ts`, `last_error_code`, `format_version`, `artifact_location` (maybe redacted), plus per-component statuses.
- If we need multi-tenant in the hosted control plane later, create a different surface (`/admin/...`).

Benji Okafor: That’s doable. Right now we support multiple tenants in the same Private control plane only in the sense of multiple “orgs” — but install is still one cluster. So “this install” endpoint works.

Marcus Lin: Tenancy isolation detail: make sure response doesn’t leak other tenants’ configured backup locations or KMS key IDs. I saw `kms_key_arn` in the response struct in the PR.

Benji Okafor: Good catch. That was meant for debugging, but we can remove or hash.

Hanae Suzuki: Please remove KMS identifiers from the API response. If we need to validate KMS integration, do it via a separate "kms health" check, not in a backup status endpoint.

Rafael Mendes: Rate limiting: can we set something like 1 rps per token, burst 5, and cache for 30s? Console might poll every page refresh + background.

Ethan Park: There’s already gateway rate limiting, but we can add a local cache. Right now handler calls BackupStatusStore (postgres) directly.

Sean Gallagher: I’d be happy with:
- 30s cache keyed by install_id
- response includes `computed_at`
- rate limit at edge (global + per token)
Also: please pick error codes we can page on. “string message” will end up in alerts.

Allison Grant: If we keep it, can we include a stable `last_error_category` enum? e.g. `STORAGE_AUTHZ`, `KMS_UNAVAILABLE`, `INTEGRITY_CHECK_FAILED`, `UNKNOWN`.

Benji Okafor: Yup. In PR it’s `last_error` freeform. I can change to `{code, category}` (but still keep it flat-ish).

Priya Natarajan: Keep it simple: `last_error_code` (stable string), `last_error_hint` (optional, human), and no stack traces.

Marcus Lin: Authz scopes: I didn’t see explicit scope checks. Please ensure:
- only control-plane service tokens with `private:ops:read` (or whatever we call it) can call
- customer admin UI sessions can call if they have cluster-admin (or Redwood Private admin role)
- deny by default
And please return 404 for “not configured” rather than 403/200 w/ empty fields? Helps avoid scanning patterns.

Ethan Park: PR currently checks `HasRole("private-operator")` for session auth, and service tokens go through mTLS allowlist. I’ll tighten scope naming + comments.

Hanae Suzuki: Also consider token audience. We’ve had issues where any internal token worked across services.

Benji Okafor: Ack. I’ll add audience validation to the middleware (we already have it for other private routes).

Sean Gallagher: One more: can we avoid putting `artifact_location` at all? That could be S3 bucket names / NFS paths. Support might want it, but it’s also sensitive.

Vivek Kulkarni: For customers, we don’t want them thinking we “manage” their bucket. Better to just show “backend: s3/nfs/gcs” + “retention: X days” and a doc link.

Marcus Lin: +1. Remove raw locations. If needed, we can include `backend_type` and `backend_id` as opaque (e.g., "default"), but no bucket names.

Benji Okafor: Makes sense. I’ll drop `artifact_location` and `kms_key_arn`. Keep:
- backend_type
- last_success_ts
- last_attempt_ts
- bytes_written
- duration_ms
- format_version
- last_error_code
- computed_at

Allison Grant: Add `job_name`? Helps correlate with metrics/logs. But ok if not.

Priya Natarajan: Let’s not add too many fields. If we need correlation, we can add `operation_id` later.

Ethan Park: Action items from this thread:
1) Remove tenant_id param; make endpoint “this install” only
2) Remove KMS key + raw artifact location
3) Add explicit scope/audience checks; deny by default
4) Add cache + rate limit notes
5) Switch last_error to stable error code/category

GitHub Bot: @Benji Okafor pushed commits to PR #24318.

Benji Okafor: Updates pushed:
- Route now `GET /private/v1/backup/status`
- Removed `tenant_id` query param
- Removed `artifact_location` + `kms_key_arn`
- Added `last_error_code` + `last_error_category`
- Added `computed_at` + in-memory 30s cache
Still need review on scope naming.

Marcus Lin: Reviewed the diff. Scope check still reads `private-operator` role. Please map that to a named scope in authz middleware (even if it’s internally implemented as a role). Otherwise docs/SEs won’t know what to request.

Hanae Suzuki: +1. Also: confirm that for air-gapped installs, auth doesn’t attempt to introspect tokens externally.

Benji Okafor: Good point — no introspection. It’s local JWT verification + audience claim check. I’ll rename to `private:ops:read` in the middleware and keep role mapping behind the scenes.

Sean Gallagher: Cache looks good. Add a note in the runbook that polling should be <= 1/min unless troubleshooting.

Ethan Park: Sounds good. Once Benji updates scope naming, I think we can LGTM.

Priya Natarajan: Please add a short comment in code why we intentionally don’t include bucket names / key IDs (security + tenancy). Prevents regression in a future “helpful debug” PR.

Benji Okafor: Will do, pushing one more commit.

GitHub Bot: PR #24318 merged into main by @Ethan Park. ✅

title: #eng-releases: 1738869001
preview:
Connor O'Brien: trying to lock a release-train slot for the rollout plan DB migration + feature flag ramp for Dedicated staged rollouts. Goal: land migration/backfill early next train, keep flag off by default. Can folks confirm readiness + any blockers?

Talia Benson: +1, we need a date for the cross-team beta enablement plan. Connor which train are you targeting?

Connor O'Brien: aiming for Fri train cut (2025.02.07). Prefer to merge by Thu EOD so we get in the bake window.

Sahana Iyer: heads up CI for redwood main has been a bit spicy today (flake in integration suite). If we’re adding migrations we’ll want green + rerun stability.

Ethan Park: migration PR is up (pr-28411). It creates `dedicated_pool_rollout_plans` + `dedicated_pool_rollout_stages` and adds state fields. Backfill script is “no-op” unless feature flag enabled (it only backfills for pools explicitly opted-in).

Benji Okafor: +1. Validation logic is in pr-28437, but migration itself is isolated. We can ship schema first.

Rafael Mendes: from SRE side: schema-only is fine, but we need (a) clear rollback story and (b) confirm no read path starts depending on new tables until flag.

Ethan Park: read path is gated. Without `dedicated_staged_rollouts` flag, control plane ignores rollout plan lookups and continues existing pool config path.

Jordan Lee: policy engine changes (pr-28476) are also gated by the same flag + require a rollout_plan_id present. So safe to ship schema earlier.

Irene Choi: infra note: no fleet changes tied to this migration. Partitioning only happens when plan is created + promoted (flagged).

Connor O'Brien: ok, proposed sequencing:
1) Train N: ship schema migration (pr-28411) + control-plane CRUD (pr-28437) behind flag, flag default OFF.

title: #eng-platform: 1738861022
preview:
Talia Benson: trying to lock down migration + backfill plan for rollout_plans schema. release train wants a slot. can we align on sequencing today?

Connor O'Brien: yep. we have a DB change window on Thu 10:00–12:00 PT for control plane. anything that needs long-running backfill needs to be *outside* the locky migration step.

Benji Okafor: current PR has new tables + columns for `dedicated_pool_rollout_plans` + `stages` + audit hooks. main question: backfill existing dedicated pools w/ a default plan row or leave nil until first use.

Ethan Park: for v1, i’d rather not create plans for every pool automatically in the migration itself. too many pools + risk of timeouts. can we do lazy-create on first write + optional async backfill for analytics?

Priya Natarajan: +1 to lazy-create. migration should be additive + fast. backfill as a job behind a flag.

Connor O'Brien: release train constraints: we can do (1) deploy w/ additive schema, (2) app code that can read/write both (or tolerate missing), (3) run backfill job throttled, (4) flip reads, (5) later tighten constraints.

Benji Okafor: ok. but some APIs assume a plan exists when operator hits “Create rollout” in Console. that flow can just create it explicitly; no need global backfill.

Jordan Lee: policy engine will query rollout plan by pool_id. if none, it should behave as “no rollout running” and route normally.

Talia Benson: so what’s the exact migration steps? need something i can paste into the release checklist.

Connor O'Brien: proposal:
1) Migration A (train Thu): create new tables + indexes, all nullable / no foreign keys that require full scan validation.
2) Deploy code that writes new records but doesn’t require them.
3) Backfill job (after deploy): only for pools that already have “legacy rollout config” (if any), otherwise skip.
4) Migration B (later train): validate FKs, add NOT NULL where needed, add unique constraints once backfill is complete.

Benji Okafor: we don’t currently have “legacy rollout config” for Dedicated. this is net-new. so backfill is basically: none.

Ethan Park: right. the only “backfill” might be for internal dogfood pools if we want default stage templates pre-created.

Talia Benson: product wants default stage set (1/10/50/100) available. does that mean a “template” table, not per-pool?

Benji Okafor: we can store defaults in code. plan creation endpoint can populate stages with defaults. no DB pre-seed required.

Connor O'Brien: that’s cleaner. avoids global write.

Sahana Iyer: heads up: if we add an index on (pool_id, state) for the plan table, that’s fine. but avoid `CONCURRENTLY` in our migration runner (it’s not supported in the current tool). so keep the index small or schedule separately.

Benji Okafor: noted. current migration creates:
- pk on plan_id
- index on pool_id
- index on (pool_id, created_at desc)
should be ok.

SchemaBot: Reminder: Migrations running in prod should complete < 60s. Avoid full-table rewrites, adding NOT NULL with default, or validating FKs during peak. See go/link/db-migrations.

Priya Natarajan: also: multi-region replication. any migration that blocks writes even briefly will cascade. so keep it additive.

Jordan Lee: what about audit log export depending on rollout_plan_id existing? if no plan exists, no audit event. ok.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: RBAC checks in promote/abort endpoints require audit logging. but DB-wise, ensure audit table can accept null rollout_plan_id? or are we using a generic audit stream?

Benji Okafor: audit events go to the central audit event emitter, not a DB FK. payload includes pool_id + rollout_plan_id if present. so no FK concerns.

Connor O'Brien: re: backfill job anyway: do we need a job to backfill `created_by` fields for plans created via early internal scripts? if we ship w/ “system” actor default, that’s fine.

Benji Okafor: for early scripts we can set actor = “system:backfill” w/ a reason. but i’d rather keep backfill optional.

Talia Benson: can we agree on “no backfill required for GA” and only a dogfood script?

Ethan Park: yes. but we do need a *data migration* for any pools that were created during beta where the config lives in pool metadata.

Benji Okafor: do we have that? i thought beta is behind flag and not in prod yet.

Connor O'Brien: there’s a staging env + a couple internal prod pools if we enabled for testing. check INT-1933 list.

Priya Natarajan: action item: identify if any prod pools have rollout fields in pool_config. if yes, we need a one-time migration script to convert.

Jordan Lee: for safety, code path can read both:
- if `pool.rollout_plan_id` exists => use it
- else if `pool.config.rollout_preview` exists => interpret it (for a limited window)
then we can drop old field later.

Sahana Iyer: if you’re adding a new column to `dedicated_pools` like `active_rollout_plan_id`, avoid NOT NULL, avoid default, keep it nullable.

Benji Okafor: we currently *don’t* add a pointer column; plan table has pool_id. policy engine queries by pool_id + state=running.

Ethan Park: good. fewer writes on hot path.

Connor O'Brien: ok, so “backfill” is basically:
- optional script: for any pool w/ rollout_preview config, create plan + stages, mark state accordingly
- run script manually off-hours, with rate limit.

Talia Benson: what’s the rate limit / batching plan? release checklist needs it.

Benji Okafor: script will:
- select pools where config has `rollout_preview`
- process in batches of 50
- sleep 200ms between batches
- max runtime 30m
- idempotent: checks if a plan already exists for pool_id w/ same change_id before creating.

Connor O'Brien: also run with `--dry-run` first and post counts in #eng-releases.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: ensure the script requires admin creds + logs actor/reason. even if it’s “system”, we need traceability.

Benji Okafor: yep, will require a `--reason` arg and will emit audit event “rollout_plan.migrated_from_legacy_config”.

Jordan Lee: policy engine side: during the conversion window, if both exist, prefer plan table. add a log line for detection.

Priya Natarajan: timeline: Migration A in Thu train. conversion script only if needed, run Fri morning. Migration B (constraints) no earlier than 2 trains later, after we confirm no legacy usage.

Connor O'Brien: works. i’ll reserve Thu train slot and add “no backfill in migration” to the release doc.

Talia Benson: can someone drop a succinct step list i can paste into the project tracker?

Connor O'Brien: 
- Train T (Thu): apply additive schema (new rollout plan tables + indexes), deploy code tolerant of missing plans.
- Post-deploy: run detection query for legacy rollout_preview config; if non-zero, run conversion script throttled + audited.
- Monitor: DB write latency + replication lag during script; abort if lag > threshold.
- Train T+2: validate constraints / tighten schema after legacy config is confirmed gone.

Benji Okafor: (reaction: :thumbsup: from Benji Okafor)

Talia Benson: (reaction: :thumbsup: from Talia Benson)

Priya Natarajan: (reaction: :thumbsup: from Priya Natarajan) + please add “rollback plan”: app can run with schema present but unused; if we revert code, tables remain harmless.

Connor O'Brien: yep. if rollback, we leave new tables, flip feature flag off, no data loss.

Sahana Iyer: one more: don’t forget to update db-permissions for the new tables in the readonly role used by some reporting jobs.

Benji Okafor: good catch, i’ll add to PR + ping you for review.

Talia Benson: closing the loop. thanks all.

title: #architecture: 1738862109
preview:
Miles Thornton: We need to settle backup artifact format stability + migration strategy before we lock docs. Specifically: do we treat backups as a semver’d API? What are our compatibility guarantees?

Michael Nguyen: +1. ENG-9830 is blocked on this. Today the installer emits `formatVersion: 1` in the manifest but it’s basically “best effort.” If we’re shipping to regulated on-prem, “oops new installer can’t restore old backup” is not acceptable.

Ethan Park: Agree on not breaking restores, but we should be explicit about scope. We’re backing up control-plane state (db + config + some k8s objects) NOT “entire cluster.” That affects what “compatibility” means.

Connor O'Brien: From release side: we can enforce at build time that backup format changes are reviewed (ADR + changelog). But we need a clear policy: what’s MAJOR vs MINOR. Also, we have to support air-gapped bundle verification; migrations can’t require “reach out to Redwood servers.”

Miles Thornton: Let’s enumerate options:
1) Backup format is versioned (semver) and restore supports N previous majors
2) Backup format is “pinned” to platform version and we ship migration utilities
3) We never change backup format (lol)

Hanae Suzuki: Security angle: any migration step that decrypts/re-encrypts is a key-handling event. Need deterministic behavior + audit logs. Also: do we keep old ciphertext as-is and only rewrite manifest? depends on what changes.

Marcus Lin: Also threat model: backups are an attacker target. Format changes often come with metadata changes. We should avoid “silent coercion” on restore. If we detect unknown fields/version, fail closed with actionable message.

Benji Okafor: Practical: restore workflow needs to decide early if it can proceed. I want something like:
- read manifest
- verify checksums
- verify signature (air-gapped)
- check `formatVersion` compatibility
- only then decrypt / import

Michael Nguyen: +1. Can we do semver on backup *format* (manifest schema + archive layout) and separately track *data migrations* (db schema)? They’re related but not the same.

Ethan Park: Yep. DB migrations already exist in control-plane. Restore is trickier because we may restore to a different platform version.

Neha Kapoor: Reliability POV: we should define a contract that makes SRE life easier:
- N-1 restore works (newest installer can restore previous minor/patch backups)
- Major bumps require explicit operator action + preflight warning
- Provide a “dry run / validate” mode that tells you exactly what will happen

Allison Grant: Observability note: we need structured events when format mismatch happens (category: compatibility) so dashboards can show how often customers are hitting it.

Miles Thornton: Ok, semver proposal:
- `backupFormatVersion` = MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH
- MAJOR: breaking layout/schema change (requires migration tool)
- MINOR: backwards compatible additions (new fields optional)
- PATCH: bugfix / clarifications (no behavior change)

Marcus Lin: I’m mostly ok with semver, but define “backwards compatible” rigorously. Example: adding new metadata is fine if restore ignores unknown keys. But do we *want* ignoring unknown keys? That’s the classic footgun.

Michael Nguyen: On restore we can do: allow unknown keys in manifest but require known `backupFormatVersion` range. Unknown keys get logged (debug). If MAJOR mismatched, error. If MINOR higher than supported, error unless we can safely ignore.

Ethan Park: That turns into: restore supports `MAJOR == X` and `MINOR <= maxMinorSupported`. That’s fine.

Connor O'Brien: But you’re describing “new backup might not restore on older installer,” which is ok. We care more about “new installer restoring old backup.”

Neha Kapoor: Exactly. Customer expectation: you can upgrade tooling and still restore the last few backups.

Miles Thornton: So compatibility direction: backward compat for restore (new tool restores old backups). Forward compat (old tool restores new backup) not required.

Hanae Suzuki: With encryption: if we change encryption envelope format, that’s a MAJOR. But we should isolate encryption metadata in manifest and keep ciphertext opaque. Ideally: encryption is “provider + parameters” and doesn’t change often.

Marcus Lin: +1. Also: manifest should include KMS key reference (arn/uri) but never include plaintext material. And we should not back up actual secret values if we can avoid it; references only (aligns w/ ADR-027).

Michael Nguyen: I’ll paste what I think the manifest should look like long term (rough):

```yaml
backupFormatVersion: 1.1.0
createdAt: 2025-02-06T19:05:12Z
platformVersion: 2025.02.1
components:
  controlPlaneDb:
    engine: postgres
    logicalDump: true
    artifact: artifacts/control-plane-db.sql.zst.enc
  k8s:
    includedNamespaces: ["redwood-system", "redwood-control-plane"]
    artifact: artifacts/k8s-resources.tar.zst.enc
crypto:
  envelope:
    provider: aws-kms
    keyRef: arn:aws:kms:us-east-1:...:key/...
  dataKeyAlg: AES-256-GCM
integrity:
  manifestSha256: ...
  artifacts:
    - path: artifacts/control-plane-db.sql.zst.enc
      sha256: ...
    - path: artifacts/k8s-resources.tar.zst.enc
      sha256: ...
compat:
  minRestoreToolVersion: 0.9.0
  maxTestedRestoreToolVersion: 1.3.2
notes: "generated by redwood-private-installer"
```

Connor O'Brien: Agree. “max tested” will rot fast and look like a promise.

Miles Thornton: Maybe:
- `createdBy.tool` + `createdBy.version`
- `required.restoreTool.minVersion` (rare)

Benji Okafor: Another detail: archive layout. Do we want deterministic layout?

Connor O'Brien: Please yes. Deterministic layout helps signing + reproducible bundles. Also in air-gapped import/export we want predictable paths.

Marcus Lin: Deterministic is good, but don’t bake secrets into path names (customer names, tenant ids). Keep it generic.

Michael Nguyen: Re: migration strategy: If we bump MAJOR, do we ship `backup migrate` that rewrites old to new?

Neha Kapoor: SRE preference: fewer migration steps during an incident. In DR you’re already stressed; “run migrate, then restore” adds risk.

Ethan Park: +1. If we can, keep restore tool able to read at least 1 previous major. But we can reserve the right not to.

Connor O'Brien: Release reality: maintaining multiple majors indefinitely is expensive. But supporting at least “current major and previous major” seems doable.

Marcus Lin: Security preference: fewer legacy parsers forever. Each parser is an attack surface.

Hanae Suzuki: If we do multi-major, enforce strict parsing + size limits (zip bombs etc). Also signature verification BEFORE extraction.

Benji Okafor: +1 to “verify before extract.” Current PR-512 does checksum verification but we should ensure we’re not untarring untrusted content prior to verifying.

Neha Kapoor: Put it in the RPO/RTO + supported versions matrix. But on restore, tool should preflight check the target environment matches requirements (db engine/version).

Allison Grant: And emit a metric/event when preflight fails due to version mismatch so we can see it.

Hanae Suzuki: Also include statement about key rotation and what happens if KMS unavailable during restore (fail, don’t fallback to plaintext).

Michael Nguyen: I’ll comment on ENG-9830 with this proposal and link it to PR-512 notes.

Miles Thornton: Cool. I’ll draft the ADR text tonight and tag folks for review.
text:
Miles Thornton: We need to settle backup artifact format stability + migration strategy before we lock docs. Specifically: do we treat backups as a semver’d API? What are our compatibility guarantees?

Michael Nguyen: +1. ENG-9830 is blocked on this. Today the installer emits `formatVersion: 1` in the manifest but it’s basically “best effort.” If we’re shipping to regulated on-prem, “oops new installer can’t restore old backup” is not acceptable.

Ethan Park: Agree on not breaking restores, but we should be explicit about scope. We’re backing up control-plane state (db + config + some k8s objects) NOT “entire cluster.” That affects what “compatibility” means.

Connor O'Brien: From release side: we can enforce at build time that backup format changes are reviewed (ADR + changelog). But we need a clear policy: what’s MAJOR vs MINOR. Also, we have to support air-gapped bundle verification; migrations can’t require “reach out to Redwood servers.”

Miles Thornton: Let’s enumerate options:
1) Backup format is versioned (semver) and restore supports N previous majors
2) Backup format is “pinned” to platform version and we ship migration utilities
3) We never change backup format (lol)

Hanae Suzuki: Security angle: any migration step that decrypts/re-encrypts is a key-handling event. Need deterministic behavior + audit logs. Also: do we keep old ciphertext as-is and only rewrite manifest? depends on what changes.

Marcus Lin: Also threat model: backups are an attacker target. Format changes often come with metadata changes. We should avoid “silent coercion” on restore. If we detect unknown fields/version, fail closed with actionable message.

Benji Okafor: Practical: restore workflow needs to decide early if it can proceed. I want something like:
- read manifest
- verify checksums
- verify signature (air-gapped)
- check `formatVersion` compatibility
- only then decrypt / import

Michael Nguyen: +1. Can we do semver on backup *format* (manifest schema + archive layout) and separately track *data migrations* (db schema)? They’re related but not the same.

Ethan Park: Yep. DB migrations already exist in control-plane. Restore is trickier because we may restore to a different platform version.

Neha Kapoor: Reliability POV: we should define a contract that makes SRE life easier:
- N-1 restore works (newest installer can restore previous minor/patch backups)
- Major bumps require explicit operator action + preflight warning
- Provide a “dry run / validate” mode that tells you exactly what will happen

Allison Grant: Observability note: we need structured events when format mismatch happens (category: compatibility) so dashboards can show how often customers are hitting it.

Miles Thornton: Ok, semver proposal:
- `backupFormatVersion` = MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH
- MAJOR: breaking layout/schema change (requires migration tool)
- MINOR: backwards compatible additions (new fields optional)
- PATCH: bugfix / clarifications (no behavior change)

Marcus Lin: I’m mostly ok with semver, but define “backwards compatible” rigorously. Example: adding new metadata is fine if restore ignores unknown keys. But do we *want* ignoring unknown keys? That’s the classic footgun.

Michael Nguyen: On restore we can do: allow unknown keys in manifest but require known `backupFormatVersion` range. Unknown keys get logged (debug). If MAJOR mismatched, error. If MINOR higher than supported, error unless we can safely ignore.

Ethan Park: That turns into: restore supports `MAJOR == X` and `MINOR <= maxMinorSupported`. That’s fine.

Connor O'Brien: But you’re describing “new backup might not restore on older installer,” which is ok. We care more about “new installer restoring old backup.”

Neha Kapoor: Exactly. Customer expectation: you can upgrade tooling and still restore the last few backups.

Miles Thornton: So compatibility direction: backward compat for restore (new tool restores old backups). Forward compat (old tool restores new backup) not required.

Hanae Suzuki: With encryption: if we change encryption envelope format, that’s a MAJOR. But we should isolate encryption metadata in manifest and keep ciphertext opaque. Ideally: encryption is “provider + parameters” and doesn’t change often.

Marcus Lin: +1. Also: manifest should include KMS key reference (arn/uri) but never include plaintext material. And we should not back up actual secret values if we can avoid it; references only (aligns w/ ADR-027).

Michael Nguyen: I’ll paste what I think the manifest should look like long term (rough):

```yaml
backupFormatVersion: 1.1.0
createdAt: 2025-02-06T19:05:12Z
platformVersion: 2025.02.1
components:
  controlPlaneDb:
    engine: postgres
    logicalDump: true
    artifact: artifacts/control-plane-db.sql.zst.enc
  k8s:
    includedNamespaces: ["redwood-system", "redwood-control-plane"]
    artifact: artifacts/k8s-resources.tar.zst.enc
crypto:
  envelope:
    provider: aws-kms
    keyRef: arn:aws:kms:us-east-1:...:key/...
  dataKeyAlg: AES-256-GCM
integrity:
  manifestSha256: ...
  artifacts:
    - path: artifacts/control-plane-db.sql.zst.enc
      sha256: ...
    - path: artifacts/k8s-resources.tar.zst.enc
      sha256: ...
compat:
  minRestoreToolVersion: 0.9.0
  maxTestedRestoreToolVersion: 1.3.2
notes: "generated by redwood-private-installer"
```

title: #eng-platform: 1738785501
preview:
Jordan Lee: We need to lock audit event schema for promote/abort (and overrides). Trying to get ahead of Console + SIEM export.

Jordan Lee: Fields we *think* we need: actor, reason, old/new stage, override flags, and where event emitted (control plane vs policy engine).

Benji Okafor: +1. I’m implementing promote/abort endpoints this week. Need to know exact payload so I don’t have to churn migrations.

Ethan Park: For Dedicated rollouts, we should have a single source of truth: control plane state machine. Emit audit events from there, not from routing/policy engine.

Hannah Rivera: Console wants to show the audit trail row for: promote, abort, hold, force-promote. Also “gate failed -> auto-abort” should show as system action.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: Security note: actor must capture both the authenticated principal and the “effective actor” if impersonation / support tools used.

Kevin Osei: + retention/export: we need stable event_type strings + a consistent “resource” tuple so the export pipeline can route it.

Jordan Lee: Ok, proposal (v0). Event types:
- dedicated_pool_rollout.plan_created
- dedicated_pool_rollout.plan_updated
- dedicated_pool_rollout.stage_promoted
- dedicated_pool_rollout.aborted
- dedicated_pool_rollout.held
- dedicated_pool_rollout.override_force_promote
- dedicated_pool_rollout.override_force_abort (do we need? or just aborted w/ override)
- dedicated_pool_rollout.override_release_hold

Benji Okafor: For the schema, can we keep it mostly consistent with existing audit envelope?
Like:
- event_id
- event_type
- ts
- actor (id, type)
- actor_display
- request_id / trace_id
- resource_type + resource_id

Kaitlyn Nguyen: Please don’t rely on actor_display for anything security-related. Nice-to-have only.
Need:
- actor_principal_id
- actor_principal_type (user/service)
- actor_org_id (if internal)
- on_behalf_of_principal_id (optional)
- auth_method (api_key, session, service_token)

Ethan Park: +1. Also include rbac_role / permission evaluated? Maybe too much.
At least include: authorization_decision_id? That’s overkill.

Jordan Lee: For promote/abort specific fields:
- rollout_plan_id
- dedicated_pool_id
- change_id (the thing we’re rolling out, e.g. model_version/runtime_build/policy)
- from_stage_id / to_stage_id
- from_stage_name / to_stage_name
- from_percent / to_percent (if percentage stages)
- from_partition / to_partition (if replica partition stages)
- rollout_state_before / rollout_state_after
- reason (freeform, required for overrides)
- override_type (none|force_promote|force_abort|hold|release_hold)

Hannah Rivera: Console also asked for “gate evidence” pointer. Not the full metrics payload, but a reference.
Maybe:
- gate_evaluation_id
- gate_summary (pass/fail/unknown + which gate tripped)

Rafael Mendes: Oncall POV: for auto-aborts we need to know which gate and the window. Even a short summary is fine.
Don’t make us click 3 different systems during an incident.

Kevin Osei: Careful on putting raw metric values into audit logs (PII risk low, but volume/cardinality). A compact summary is okay.
Also: the export job expects event payload size under ~16KB.

Benji Okafor: Where does gate_evaluation_id live? In policy engine or telemetry?

Jordan Lee: In policy engine (gate evaluator) we generate an evaluation record id. Control plane queries it (or gets it pushed) and makes the state transition.
So audit event should be emitted at the moment control plane persists new state, with gate_evaluation_id attached.

Ethan Park: Strong preference: policy engine should not emit audit events. Otherwise we get duplicates when promote triggers eval and then CP applies transition.
We can log internal events in PE for debugging, but “audit” should be CP only.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: Agree. “Audit” == authoritative, deterministic, minimal. Emit on state transition.
But please include:
- idempotency_key (if present)
- optimistic_concurrency_version (or etag) used
So we can reconcile double-clicks / retries.

Benji Okafor: I can include:
- idempotency_key
- request_id
- plan_version_before / plan_version_after (int)

Jordan Lee: What about “who/what triggered the change” for auto events?
I propose:
- trigger: manual|automatic
- trigger_source: console|api|policy_engine
If automatic, actor_principal_type=service and actor_principal_id="policy-engine"?

Rafael Mendes: + include “runbook_url” optional? lol
(jk but a link would be nice)

Hannah Rivera: +1 on trigger_source. Console wants to show “Auto-aborted by gates” vs “Aborted by user”.

Kevin Osei: For SIEM, resource keys matter. Suggest we standardize:
- resource_type: "dedicated_pool_rollout_plan"
- resource_id: rollout_plan_id
And include related:
- related_resource_types: dedicated_pool, account
- related_resource_ids

Kaitlyn Nguyen: + account_id is mandatory. We need tenant boundary in every event.

Benji Okafor: ok. Adding:
- account_id
- dedicated_pool_id
- region (optional?)

Ethan Park: Region is tricky since plan spans pool which may be multi-region. Maybe:
- regions_affected: comma-separated list
Or leave out of audit, keep in rollout plan record.

Jordan Lee: I’d keep:
- regions_affected
as a string like "iad, fra".

Kaitlyn Nguyen: Works as long as we’re not putting anything customer-identifying beyond their account_id (which is already the tenant id).

Jordan Lee: Another question: include old/new config diff? e.g. model version old->new.

Kevin Osei: I’d avoid a full diff. But include identifiers:
- change_kind: model_version|runtime_build|routing_policy|config
- change_ref_old
- change_ref_new
No big blobs.

Benji Okafor: That maps well to our plan model anyway.

Hannah Rivera: Also for hold/release hold, stage doesn’t change but state does. Should audit include stage_id still? so UI can show “held at 10%”.

Jordan Lee: yes:
- current_stage_id
- current_stage_name

Rafael Mendes: For abort, do we capture “rollback target stage”? We’ve talked about reverting to previous stable.

Ethan Park: For v1 abort semantics: abort returns routing to baseline (stage 0 / stable partition), and marks rollout aborted.
So include:
- abort_target_stage_id (probably "stable")

Jordan Lee: ok.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: One more thing: “reason” is user-provided text. Sanitize/limit length.
And make it required for override_type != none.

Benji Okafor: ✅ I’ll enforce max 280 chars and strip newlines.

Jordan Lee: Drafting final field list. Pasting as pseudo:

Jordan Lee: ```
required:
- event_id
- event_type
- ts
- account_id
- actor_principal_id
- actor_principal_type (user|service)
- auth_method
- request_id
- resource_type (dedicated_pool_rollout_plan)
- resource_id (rollout_plan_id)
- dedicated_pool_id
- rollout_state_before
- rollout_state_after

optional (but strongly recommended):
- on_behalf_of_principal_id
- on_behalf_of_principal_type
- idempotency_key
- plan_version_before
- plan_version_after
- trigger (manual|automatic)
- trigger_source (console|api|policy_engine)
- reason
- override_type
- from_stage_id
- to_stage_id
- current_stage_id
- stage_representation (percent|replica_partition)
- from_allocation
- to_allocation
- abort_target_stage_id
- change_kind
- change_ref_old
- change_ref_new
- gate_evaluation_id
- gate_summary
- regions_affected
```

Kevin Osei: Looks good. For gate_summary keep it a string, e.g. "FAIL latency_p99 i.e. 920ms > 750ms (15m window)".

Rafael Mendes: + include "UNKNOWN" when missing metrics. That matters.

Jordan Lee: Right, and if missing metrics caused auto-hold (if we implement) then event should say it.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: Where will events be emitted precisely?
Need to confirm: CP emits after DB write succeeds. Not before.

Benji Okafor: Plan is:
- promote endpoint validates, evaluates gate preconditions, then writes state transition (within tx)
- after commit, emit audit event
For auto-abort, policy engine calls CP "Abort" API (service identity), CP writes + emits.

Ethan Park: Great. That gives exactly-once *ish* semantics; in practice may need dedupe in exporter.
We should include event_id uuid generated at persistence time.

Kevin Osei: exporter dedupes on event_id today, so yes please.

Hannah Rivera: Any concern about users seeing internal actor_principal_id like "policy-engine"? Console audit view will need a friendly label.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: That’s fine as long as it’s consistent and not secret. Also: don’t leak internal emails in customer-facing UI.

Jordan Lee: ok, we’ll map service ids to friendly names in Console.

Benji Okafor: I’ll update PR with this schema + add TODO for any fields we can’t populate in v1 (regions_affected maybe).

Priya Natarajan: jumping in late. This looks reasonable. Please make sure we don’t end up with 3 similar event_types (aborted vs override_force_abort). Prefer one event_type + override_type if possible.

Jordan Lee: +1, simplify:
- dedicated_pool_rollout.aborted
and include override_type=force_abort when applicable.
Same for promote.

Kevin Osei: Works. Event types should be stable + low cardinality.

Rafael Mendes: Also: add a note in runbook: if you force-promote, include incident link in reason.

Benji Okafor: will do.

Jordan Lee: Closing this thread: emitting from control plane on state transitions, schema as above, one event_type per action + override_type for overrides. I’ll post final in Confluence + link from the PR.

Jordan Lee: ok.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: One more thing: “reason” is user-provided text. Sanitize/limit length.
And make it required for override_type != none.

Benji Okafor: ✅ I’ll enforce max 280 chars and strip newlines.

Jordan Lee: Drafting final field list. Pasting as pseudo:

Jordan Lee: ```
required:
- event_id
- event_type
- ts
- account_id
- actor_principal_id
- actor_principal_type (user|service)
- auth_method
- request_id
- resource_type (dedicated_pool_rollout_plan)
- resource_id (rollout_plan_id)
- dedicated_pool_id
- rollout_state_before
- rollout_state_after

optional (but strongly recommended):
- on_behalf_of_principal_id
- on_behalf_of_principal_type
- idempotency_key
- plan_version_before
- plan_version_after
- trigger (manual|automatic)
- trigger_source (console|api|policy_engine)
- reason
- override_type
- from_stage_id
- to_stage_id
- current_stage_id
- stage_representation (percent|replica_partition)
- from_allocation
- to_allocation
- abort_target_stage_id
- change_kind
- change_ref_old
- change_ref_new
- gate_evaluation_id
- gate_summary
- regions_affected
```

Kevin Osei: Looks good. For gate_summary keep it a string, e.g. "FAIL latency_p99 i.e. 920ms > 750ms (15m window)".

Rafael Mendes: + include "UNKNOWN" when missing metrics. That matters.

Jordan Lee: Right, and if missing metrics caused auto-hold (if we implement) then event should say it.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: Where will events be emitted precisely?
Need to confirm: CP emits after DB write succeeds. Not before.

Benji Okafor: Plan is:
- promote endpoint validates, evaluates gate preconditions, then writes state transition (within tx)
- after commit, emit audit event
For auto-abort, policy engine calls CP "Abort" API (service identity), CP writes + emits.

Ethan Park: Great. That gives exactly-once *ish* semantics; in practice may need dedupe in exporter.
We should include event_id uuid generated at persistence time.

Kevin Osei: exporter dedupes on event_id today, so yes please.

Hannah Rivera: Any concern about users seeing internal actor_principal_id like "policy-engine"? Console audit view will need a friendly label.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: That’s fine as long as it’s consistent and not secret. Also: don’t leak internal emails in customer-facing UI.

Jordan Lee: ok, we’ll map service ids to friendly names in Console.

Benji Okafor: I’ll update PR with this schema + add TODO for any fields we can’t populate in v1 (regions_affected maybe).

Priya Natarajan: jumping in late. This looks reasonable. Please make sure we don’t end up with 3 similar event_types (aborted vs override_force_abort). Prefer one event_type + override_type if possible.

Jordan Lee: +1, simplify:
- dedicated_pool_rollout.aborted
and include override_type=force_abort when applicable.
Same for promote.

Kevin Osei: Works. Event types should be stable + low cardinality.

Rafael Mendes: Also: add a note in runbook: if you force-promote, include incident link in reason.

Benji Okafor: will do.

Jordan Lee: Closing this thread: emitting from control plane on state transitions, schema as above, one event_type per action + override_type for overrides. I’ll post final in Confluence + link from the PR.

title: #architecture: 1738765432
preview:
Priya Natarajan: quick RFC follow-up — thinking about making rollout metadata intentionally minimal on the wire and doing client-delta probing from the console. TL;DR: small pings for compatibility, server-side enrichment for ops. tradeoffs?
Ben Cho: +1 to minimalism, but worried about post-rollback debugging. we should keep at least: model, variant, pin_reason, expires_at/soft_ttl. otherwise ops blind.
Mateo Alvarez: +1. also how do we distinguish deprecation vs soft pin? suggest a compact enum like retire_hint: 'none'|'warn'|'soft_retire'|'hard_retire' so clients can surface warnings.
Priya Natarajan: one option. my proposal: wire payload stays tiny, something like:
```json
{"model":"redwood/llama-70b","variant":"q4o","pin_mode":"soft","expires_at":"2026-04-01T12:00:00Z"}
```
control plane enriches with deprecation hints, computed priority, origin, etc., only when a client opts in or for console UI.
Lina (eng-bot): deploy-bot: rollout-schema-v2 pushed to staging, schema validation tests: 45/50 passed, failing cases are optional fields parsing.
Ben Cho: practical concern — older SDKs will ignore expires_at. we need server-side grace/fallback window (eg: 72h default) + tenant override. otherwise pins could silently linger.
Mateo Alvarez: agreed. also when overlapping pins occur we need deterministic resolver rules (priority > created_ts > origin). should we encode 'priority' on-wire or keep it server-computed?
Priya Natarajan: prefer server-computed priority to keep wire small. but we can include a small optional debug view: origin and computed_priority when clients opt-in to richer payloads. that keeps default clients lean.
Ben Cho: sounds good. propose two small optional fields: "origin" (string) and "computed_priority" (int). optional, so normal clients ignore them. keeps debugging feasible.
Mateo Alvarez: telemetry-wise we should attach a tiny "delta_probe" sample: {last_seen_version, last_probe_ts, sdk_version}. very small, helps migration metrics.
Priya Natarajan: deprecation semantics need to be explicit in ops playbook: warn -> console alert only; soft_retire -> attempt autoreplace with compatible variant after N failures; hard_retire -> block new pins and trigger pager. include tenant/SLA exemptions for autoreplace.
Ben Cho: add constraint: autoreplace must respect tenant pin overrides and SLA tier — premium tenants opt-out of auto-migrations.
Mateo Alvarez: question for private installs: if control plane enriches metadata, should we sign that enrichment so on-host components can trust it?
Priya Natarajan: yes. for Private we should sign control-plane-issued enrichment payloads. hosted infra can rely on internal auth, but signature optional field for private installs.
Ben Cho: action items: (1) draft minimal wire schema doc + example payloads, (2) define server enrichment fields + signing rules, (3) implement grace window/fallback behavior, (4) add delta_probe telemetry proto, (5) update SDKs to parse optional debug fields.
Priya Natarajan: I'll draft the PR for the wire schema + example payloads and ping @Mateo Alvarez and @Ben Cho for review. thanks all.

title: #eng-sre: 1738637750
preview:
Sean Gallagher: We need to lock down *standard* gate thresholds + templates for Dedicated staged rollouts. Right now every rollout doc/thread has different numbers and we’re going to end up with noisy alerts + inconsistent promote decisions.

Sean Gallagher: Can we align on v1 defaults this week? At minimum: error rate, p95/p99 latency, saturation/headroom, OOM rate, 429/drop, GPU health. Plus: evaluation window + minimum sample size.

Rafael Mendes: +1. Also want a clear stance on “per-customer variability” — we can’t tune per account in v1 or we’ll never scale oncall.

Peter Holtz: Agree. If we do fixed numbers that are too strict we’ll just stall rollouts forever (then operators force-promote and we lose safety). If too lax, gates won’t catch regressions. Need to tie to SLO + error budget.

Paula Mendes: (looping in) I’m drafting defaults for PM-827 but need SRE signoff. Question: should we template by “tier” (standard vs premium Dedicated) or by “pool size / throughput class” too?

Allison Grant: From observability side: we need gate queries that are stable + not overly sensitive to traffic spikes. Also keep cardinality low. If gate is “p99” with tiny sample sizes it’s going to flap.

Logan Wright: On telemetry: some metrics are 1–2m delayed depending on region + backpressure. If the gate windows are 5m and we require continuous data, we’ll hit “unknown” a lot.

Sean Gallagher: What I’d like:
- Standard templates per gate type
- Default thresholds by tier
- Minimum N requests *and* staleness detection
- Explicit “unknown” handling (fail-closed w/ manual override?)

Rafael Mendes: Re: unknown handling: fail-open makes me nervous. During telemetry impairment we’d basically be promoting blind.

Priya Natarajan: We already have leadership leaning toward fail-closed + override for missing metrics (w/ audit). But we need guardrails so we don’t block all rollouts during a telemetry incident.

Peter Holtz: Maybe policy:
- If gates are unknown due to telemetry staleness, state becomes “blocked: missing data” and requires human action (hold or force-promote)
- Force-promote requires admin + typed reason + ticket link

Paula Mendes: That maps to what product wants in Console: “Gate status: PASS/FAIL/UNKNOWN” + show “why unknown” (stale, no samples, query error)

Allison Grant: Concrete suggestions:
- Error rate gate: use 5xx + model-runtime errors (exclude client 4xx) over rolling 10m, require >= 500 req for p95/p99 evaluation (?)
- Latency: p95 and p99 over 10m, compare *delta* vs baseline? (but baseline is hard)

Rafael Mendes: Baseline comparisons scare me for v1. Baseline shifts w/ time of day + workload mix. I’d rather do absolute ceilings tied to Dedicated SLO.

Sean Gallagher: Dedicated SLO for most pools is 99.9% and p95 < X ms (varies by model). We can’t bake model-specific thresholds into a generic gate template unless we key off the pool’s SLO config.

Mina Haddad: Routing perspective: stage partitions can reduce effective capacity, so we should have a “saturation/headroom” gate that catches queue depth spikes before latency melts.

Vanessa Ortiz: +1. Fleet metrics we can provide reliably:
- GPU util (smoothed)
- queue depth / scheduler backlog
- OOM events per replica
- unhealthy GPU/node count (ECC, Xid, taints)
But note: small pools have noisy utilization; need “min replica count per stage” or we’ll get false saturation.

Irene Choi: Capacity guideline draft says canary stage should be at least 2 replicas per region for Dedicated, otherwise you’re basically single-node canary and you’ll see 429s if that node hiccups.

Peter Holtz: On 429/drop: should be a hard gate. If the stage introduces 429s above baseline, that’s a regression for customers.

Sean Gallagher: Proposed v1 gate set (defaults):
1) Error rate: (5xx + internal) / total <= 0.5% over 10m, min 1k req
2) 429 rate: <= 0.2% over 10m, min 1k req
3) p95 latency: <= SLO_p95 * 1.2 over 10m, min 500 req
4) p99 latency: <= SLO_p99 * 1.2 over 10m, min 500 req
5) Saturation/headroom: queue depth p95 <= threshold, GPU util p95 <= 90% (or headroom >= 10%)
6) OOM rate: 0 over 10m (or <= 1 per 10m?)
7) GPU health: unhealthy replicas <= 0 (or <= 1?)
Thoughts?

Rafael Mendes: I like tying latency to pool SLO instead of absolute ms. For error rate, 0.5% feels high for “gate” (that’s 99.5%). Maybe gate should be stricter than SLO? But we also want room for noise.

Allison Grant: If you use 10m windows, 0.5% on low traffic can still be 1–2 errors and looks scary. We need the min request count you listed, and maybe also min error count to avoid divide-by-small-N weirdness.

Logan Wright: Implementation detail: we can compute as rate() over 10m but we should also require “samples >= X” and “data fresh within 2m”. Otherwise it should be UNKNOWN not FAIL.

Paula Mendes: For MVP, can we ship with:
- Standard templates + default thresholds for “Standard Dedicated” and “Premium Dedicated”
- Pool config provides SLO_p95/p99
- Saturation gate uses queue depth + GPU util headroom
- OOM gate = 0 (strict)
- GPU health gate = 0 critical events (ECC/Xid)
And let customers request overrides via support (not self-serve)

Peter Holtz: OOM=0 might be too strict if we have occasional startup OOMs unrelated to steady state. Could define OOM rate threshold with exclusion window? E.g., ignore first 2 minutes after stage start.

Vanessa Ortiz: +1. We see transient OOMs during warmup / KV cache sizing changes. If the gate runs immediately it will fail even if steady state is fine.

Sean Gallagher: Good call. Let’s define a “warmup period” per stage before gates evaluate. Ex: first 3m after promotion = collecting, then evaluate over next 10m.

Rafael Mendes: That also helps alert noise. Don’t page on expected warmup blips. But if warmup exceeds X minutes, mark as suspicious.

Allison Grant: If we do warmup, please make it explicit in dashboards (stage timeline annotation: “warming up”) so people don’t think gates are broken.

Priya Natarajan: Action items:
- Sean/Rafael: propose final default thresholds + window/sample/warmup rules (by EOD tomorrow)
- Paula: incorporate into PM-827 + PRD language “limited config”
- Logan/Allison: sanity check queries + staleness detection and confirm telemetry can support the required breakdowns
- Vanessa/Irene/Mina: confirm saturation + partitioning guardrails so small pools don’t fail by design

Sean Gallagher: Works. I’ll put a doc stub in SRE space + link here. Also: alerts routing — can we make gate FAIL create a ticket first (not page) unless it’s 429/error budget burn?

Rafael Mendes: +1. Page only if:
- gate fail implies active customer impact (429 spike, error rate spike) OR
- rollout is auto-aborting and we need someone to confirm capacity is stable
Otherwise ticket + Slack notification.

Logan Wright: Re: “error budget burn” gate — if we want that in v1, we need to define how to compute burn per pool reliably. Might be v2.

Paula Mendes: For v1, we can keep “error budget aware promotion” as a policy note (no promote during incident) but not a computed gate.

Allison Grant: :thumbsup: as long as we standardize the queries and keep them consistent across Console and alerting.

Sean Gallagher: Cool. Expect a draft template shortly. Please pile on with concerns about thresholds being too aggressive/too loose for real Dedicated traffic.

Peter Holtz: One more: please ensure templates distinguish between *stage* metrics and *overall pool* metrics. We need to gate on canary partition signals, but also watch overall to avoid starving baseline traffic.

Mina Haddad: Yup — routing will emit stage_id tags for “candidate” vs “baseline” partitions. But we need a clear allowlist of labels or we’ll blow up cardinality again.

Allison Grant: Noted. We’ll keep dashboards at stage_id + region + pool_id only, no per-model-request tags.

Sean Gallagher: Great. Closing loop once draft is in.

title: #eng-platform: 1738624112
preview:
Ethan Park: trying to lock down rollout plan state machine today so we can finish API shape + migrations. current proposal: `DRAFT -> RUNNING -> COMPLETED` and `RUNNING <-> HELD`, `RUNNING|HELD -> ABORTED`. terminal = COMPLETED/ABORTED. anything missing?

Benji Okafor: +1. question: do we need an intermediate `ABORTING` or `PROMOTING` state for UI? or just model actions as events and keep state simple.

Jordan Lee: strongly prefer simple state, and represent “in progress” via action_id / audit event status. promote/abort must be idempotent anyway so having `PROMOTING` can create weird stuck states when clients retry.

Chloe Martin: from console perspective we do need to show “promotion in progress” sometimes (user clicks promote, spinner, etc). but that can be client-side / action endpoint returns immediately. if we need server-side progress, we can expose `last_action` fields without new top-level state.

Priya Natarajan: agree with Jordan—avoid extra steady states. we should keep state machine as the “source of truth” and treat actions as events that mutate state atomically.

Rafael Mendes: SRE angle: having `ABORTING` makes oncall think something is still changing when it might already have reverted traffic. for v1 we want deterministic semantics: abort = stage routing reverts to baseline (previous stable) and plan becomes terminal.

Ethan Park: ok, no ABORTING/PROMOTING state.

Ethan Park: next: transitions + constraints. DRAFT should be editable. once RUNNING, edits restricted? (e.g., can’t change stages, only description/notes?)

Benji Okafor: PR-28411 assumed: DRAFT fully editable. RUNNING: only allow `hold`, `abort`, `promote`. stage definitions immutable once running to keep auditability + prevent “move the goalposts” during gate evaluation.

Jordan Lee: +1 immutable once RUNNING. if someone needs different stages, they create a new plan revision (new rollout_plan_id) and start over. policy engine caching will be simpler too.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: RBAC needs to align with those actions. if RUNNING can’t be edited, we reduce the surface for “silent changes”. also easier to reason about what needs audit events (create/update vs promote/abort/hold).

Ethan Park: good.

Ethan Park: about HELD: should HELD be a state or just a flag? earlier thread said we need explicit “freeze/hold” state.

Jordan Lee: HELD as explicit state is fine. semantics: routing stays at current_stage, but “auto” evaluation/promotion is disabled, and manual promote is disallowed unless we explicitly allow it.

Chloe Martin: console wants: HELD shows “resume” CTA and explanation. also we want “hold” to stop auto-abort if gate signals are flapping? (at least stops promotion). but abort should still be available.

Rafael Mendes: hold shouldn’t mask gate failures. if a gate is failing hard, we still want the page/ticket. hold is more for “pause while investigating” not “ignore signals”.

Priya Natarajan: propose: gate evaluation continues and evidence keeps updating, but promotion is blocked. abort always available.

Jordan Lee: yes. HELD blocks state transitions except `resume` (HELD->RUNNING) and `abort`.

Ethan Park: ok: HELD blocks `promote`.

Benji Okafor: minor: should we allow promote from HELD if it’s a force-promote admin action? (since force actions exist)

Kaitlyn Nguyen: if force-promote exists, it should be explicit as a different permission and should work from HELD (otherwise oncall will have to “resume” first and that creates extra audit noise). but we need guardrails.

Jordan Lee: agreed. normal promote disallowed in HELD, but `force_promote` allowed if role permits.

Ethan Park: makes sense.

Ethan Park: idempotency semantics for promote/abort: suggestion is require `Idempotency-Key` header + optimistic concurrency via `If-Match: <etag>` or `expected_version` in body.

Jordan Lee: please do both. idempotency key prevents duplicate transitions on retry. expected_version prevents concurrent promotes from two clients.

Benji Okafor: implementation sketch:
- rollout_plan has `version` int incremented on every mutation
- `POST /rollout-plans/{id}:promote` requires `idempotency_key` and `expected_version`

Ethan Park: good call.

Jordan Lee: strongly prefer simple state, and represent “in progress” via action_id / audit event status. promote/abort must be idempotent anyway so having `PROMOTING` can create weird stuck states when clients retry.

title: #eng-platform: 1738013377
preview:
Ethan Park: We need a control-plane endpoint that reports backup readiness/status for Private. Question: do we stick it into existing control-plane API service, or spin up a new “backup subsystem” service?

Vivek Kulkarni: +1. Customers are asking “prove to me backups are running” and “what’s last good backup” without SSH’ing into k8s. Console also needs something consistent.

Benji Okafor: Current installer `backup create` prints status locally, but for scheduled CronJob we don’t have a nice remote view other than scraping logs/metrics.

Allison Grant: From obs side, we’ll have metrics + dashboards, but UI/automation wants an API response with last_success_ts + last_error + duration. Metrics alone won’t satisfy audits.

Priya Natarajan: Preference: minimal surface area, no new service unless necessary. What’s the cleanest place in the control plane today?

Ethan Park: Options I see:
1) Add GET endpoint to `private-control-plane-api` (existing)
2) Add a new internal service `backup-controller` w/ its own API
3) Expose via the installer as an “ops endpoint” (meh)

Sean Gallagher: Please no new service unless we have to. Every new deployment unit becomes an oncall liability. But we also need to not bolt random endpoints onto the main API without authz/tenancy thought.

Marcus Lin: Security lens: endpoint must not leak sensitive artifact locations (bucket names, object keys) or key IDs. Also needs proper authz scope; avoid “cluster-admin can see everything” being the only answer.

Hanae Suzuki: Also if we expose “encryption provider” details, keep it high-level (e.g., kms|hsm|local) not specific key ARNs. Some customers consider those sensitive.

Michael Nguyen: What’s the source of truth for status? If backups are created by installer/CronJob, we need a persisted status record in the control-plane datastore, otherwise API is just guessing.

Benji Okafor: In PR-482 we already generate a manifest and write a summary JSON locally. For CronJob we can have it POST status to control plane, but that’s yet another auth path.

Ethan Park: We could store status as a CR in the cluster (e.g., `BackupRun`), and the API reads it via k8s API. But then API service needs RBAC + reliability considerations.

Sergio Costa: If we’re already emitting metrics (PR-24344), can API just read from metrics store?

Allison Grant: Please no. Metrics are best-effort, retention differs, and you can’t rely on Prometheus being present/healthy in every air-gapped install. Also audits want “system of record” not scraped counters.

Vivek Kulkarni: For customer story: “Console shows last successful backup and last restore drill result.” That implies persisted state.

Priya Natarajan: +1 on persisted state. Let’s avoid coupling to Prom. Ethan/Michael, can we persist backup job status in the existing config DB?

Michael Nguyen: Yes, easiest is a `backup_runs` table (or reuse existing “ops events” table if we have one). But we need to define:
- what generates it (CronJob via signed token?)
- idempotency
- retention (don’t grow DB forever)

Sean Gallagher: For SRE, we mainly need: last success timestamp, consecutive failures, last failure reason. We can expire old rows, keep the latest N.

Marcus Lin: Whatever ingests status must be authenticated. CronJob running in-cluster could call API using service account token + audience-scoped JWT. But we need to make sure SA has minimal permissions.

Hanae Suzuki: Also in air-gapped, KMS might be “local provider” or HSM. Endpoint should not block if KMS is temporarily unavailable.

Ethan Park: On “new subsystem”: if we add a `backup-controller` service, it can own status ingestion + API. But I agree with Sean: new service = more ops.

Benji Okafor: Existing `private-control-plane-api` already handles tenant config. Adding `GET /v1/ops/backup/status` seems straightforward. Ingestion could be `POST /v1/ops/backup/runs` called from CronJob.

Marcus Lin: Naming nit: avoid `/admin` unless it’s truly admin. Also need tenancy isolation: is this per “private deployment” (single-tenant)? In Private it’s basically one customer, but there may be multiple Redwood “tenants” inside.

Ethan Park: Right. We can scope it to “control plane instance” not end-user tenant. Authz can be “operator” role only (same as other private ops endpoints).

Allison Grant: Response schema suggestion:
```
{
  "enabled": true,
  "last_success_ts": "2025-01-27T18:02:11Z",
  "last_attempt_ts": "2025-01-27T18:32:09Z",
  "last_status": "SUCCESS"|"FAILED"|"RUNNING"|"UNKNOWN",
  "consecutive_failures": 0,
  "last_error_category": "RBAC"|"STORAGE"|"ENCRYPTION"|"VALIDATION"|"INTERNAL"|null,
  "last_backup_id": "bkp_...",
  "format_version": "1"
}
```
No URIs, no bucket names.

Sergio Costa: +1. If we have `last_error_category`, I can map that to “actionable” UI states and reduce noisy alerts.

Sean Gallagher: Please include `next_scheduled_ts` only if it’s reliable. We’ve had CronJobs drift in some on-prem clusters due to clock skew.

Benji Okafor: Bruno mentioned clock skew issues in on-prem. Maybe omit `next_scheduled_ts` for now.

Michael Nguyen: If we do ingestion via CronJob POST, we can include `started_at`, `finished_at`, `bytes_written` (optional), and compute duration. But do we want bytes? Might reveal growth patterns.

Marcus Lin: Bytes are usually fine, but treat as “operational telemetry.” If we include it, ensure it’s not linked to any object key. Also consider making it optional behind a flag.

Priya Natarajan: Decision: keep it in existing `private-control-plane-api` for now. No new service. Create a small “backup status” module + DB table. If it grows, we can split later.

Ethan Park: Works for me. Next: where do we put the controller logic? CronJob already runs backup. It can call API with service account credentials. Minimal additional moving parts.

Hanae Suzuki: We should define behavior when API is down during backup. CronJob should still succeed; it can retry POST later or write a local status file.

Sean Gallagher: +1. Don’t make backup success depend on reporting success. For SRE, endpoint can show UNKNOWN if no report in X hours; metrics can still page on CronJob failure.

Allison Grant: Also include `last_report_ts` so we can detect “reporting broken” vs “backup broken”.

Ethan Park: Good call.

Vivek Kulkarni: Will this unblock Console? We can render readiness for audits and for DR checklists.

Ethan Park: Yes. I’ll draft an API contract and link it to PR-24318 (status endpoints). We’ll keep the endpoint operator-only and document that it’s for Private ops, not end-user apps.

Marcus Lin: Please tag security for review once the authz scope is in. Also note: rate limit this endpoint so it can’t be polled at 1Hz by accident.

Sergio Costa: What’s a sane rate limit? Console can poll every 30-60s.

Sean Gallagher: 1 req/10s per operator token is plenty. Also cache the response for a few seconds server-side.

Priya Natarajan: Great. Ethan, can you write up a short design note + open questions (retention, schema versioning, error categories)?

Ethan Park: Yep. Action items:
- Ethan: draft endpoint + schema + authz, add to ENG-9826/PR-24318
- Michael: propose DB table + retention policy (keep last N runs + last summary)
- Benji: update backup CronJob to POST status w/ retries
- Marcus/Hanae: review auth + data exposure

Vivek Kulkarni: :thumbsup: Please keep the “customer story” in mind: screenshot-able evidence for auditors, and deterministic wording.

Allison Grant: If you add `format_version`, align it w/ backup artifact versioning so we can sanity check restore compatibility later.

Ethan Park: Will do. I’ll post a doc link in-thread once it’s ready.

title: #eng-security: 1734128045
preview:
Priya Natarajan: quick q on audit log export requests  are we allowing cross-region sinks (e.g., us-east hosted exporting to eu-west bucket) if customer asks?

Connor OBrien: we *shouldnt* by default. it breaks residency expectations + makes troubleshooting worse.

Evelyn Hart: +1. also connector in us-east assuming role into eu-west bucket has weird IAM + endpoint behavior.

Vanessa Ortiz: policy: default no cross-region. if customer insists, we need Legal + Security signoff + explicit customer ack in the ticket.

Priya Natarajan: ok. what about multi-region customers w/ separate prefixes per region?

Vanessa Ortiz: thats fine. keep export regional; separate prefixes (or buckets) per region.

Connor OBrien: also note: for Azure, cross-region storage accounts can be global-ish, but still treat as residency-impacting unless Legal clears.

Priya Natarajan: got it. Ill add to controls page + link here in the runbooks. :thumbsup:

title: #devex: 1688765432
preview:
maya rao: Heads up — console sparklines are showing weird quota spikes for a subset of tenants after yesterday's telemetry pipeline tweak. Looks like header normalization mismatch between older SDKs and the new ingest path.
jonathan li: saw this in dashboards. Which header is the culprit? route attribution or a sampling flag?
sumi patel: it's the route attribution header. Some clients send `X-Route-Meta`, others `x-route-meta` and the pipeline normalization created duplicate buckets, so aggregation counts blew up for small tenants. :confused:
priya natarajan: oh no. We rolled a Python SDK change last week that normalizes casing — did we forget to coordinate with the ingest mapping?
luke chen: server-side expects case-insensitive, but the aggregation job partitions on the raw header string before normalization. So duplicates persist in the short window.
maya rao: proposal: (1) quick console aggregation patch to normalize headers on ingest (short-term), (2) keep tolerant mapping for 14 days while we run a reconciliation job, (3) SDKs publish opt-in header `X-Route-Normalize` so we can flip gradually.
jonathan li: does that affect quota math? I worry about double charging if we merge buckets later.
sumi patel: quota attribution currently splits by raw header value. If we merge later, we'll need to re-aggregate usage to correct historical billing windows. I'll prepare a dry-run query to compute the delta first.
priya natarajan: SDK snippet for opt-in (Python):
```
# python-sdk example - opt in to normalized routing header
from redwood import Client
client = Client(api_key=..., opts={})
headers = {
  'X-Route-Meta': normalize(route_meta),
  'X-Route-Normalize': 'true'  # opt-in flag for server-side safe-merge
}
resp = client.request('/v1/generate', payload, headers=headers)
```
luke chen: TS/Go will mirror this with the opt-in header and add a telemetry event `sdk.route_normalize_optin`. We'll gate default=off for canary.
deploy-bot: Deploy scheduled: console-agg patch -> staging tonight 22:00 UTC. Logs will be retained for 72h. :rocket:
maya rao: DevEx: can you handle a short changelog/migration note? We need to tell customers that console dedupe will be applied for 14 days and that they should opt in early to avoid surprises.
jonathan li: can we do a 5% canary via routing policy? Funnel a random 5% of hosted requests through the normalization pipeline to measure delta on cost & latency.
sumi patel: yes — smart routing can mark traffic by tenant-hash. I'll add a sampling policy and a metric to watch: `quota.attribution.merge_delta`.
priya natarajan: opening SDK PRs now, will link. I will also add tests and quickstart examples showing `X-Route-Normalize`.
luke chen: remember to bump minor versions in all client libs and add a short migration guide for customers who inspect raw headers.
priya natarajan: PRs: https://github.com/redwood-inference/python-sdk/pull/456 (draft). TS & Go PRs incoming in next 2 hours.
jonathan li: once the reconciliation job is ready, I'll run it in dry-run mode and publish results in #eng-metrics before any billing adjustments.
maya rao: timeline: console patch tonight (staging), SDK canaries tomorrow, reconciliation + merge in 14 days. Any objections?
luke chen: none from me. Just ensure telemetry events include old_header/new_header mapping for traceability.
priya natarajan: added a small telemetry event in the PR: `sdk.route_header_migration: {old: 'x-route-meta', new: 'X-Route-Meta', opted_in: true}`.
sumi patel: I'll run the dry-run queries and post numbers by EOD. Also adding a reconciliation playbook for SRE in case quotas spike during merge.
maya rao: thanks all. tagging @prod-safety and @billing to watch the 14-day window. :eyes:
